skill.name: seaborn
skill.description: Seaborn guidance for statistical visualization, exploratory analysis, publication figures, correlation heatmaps, regression plots, and faceted chart design.
skill.summary: Create clear statistical visualizations with seaborn and matplotlib.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review found that the static external command, weak crypto, and reconnaissance findings are false positives caused by Markdown backticks, Python code fences, plotting terms, and hex color values. No prompt injection, hidden shell execution, credential handling, network exfiltration, or malicious intent was found in the reviewed skill files. The skill is safe to publish with low residual risk because it is community documentation that may guide users to run plotting code on local data.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported Ruby backtick execution, but the cited content is Markdown inline code and fenced Python examples for seaborn plotting. The reviewed examples import plotting libraries, build charts, and save figures; they do not instruct hidden shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation code fences or inline API names, not executable Ruby backticks. Semantic review found no shell command examples or command injection path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Crypto and Reconnaissance Terms Are Plotting Vocabulary
low_findings.description: The high and low blocker findings map to seaborn vocabulary, color palette names, hex color literals, regression examples, and grid method names. No cryptographic API use, password hashing, host discovery, port scanning, or system reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines describe palettes, plot grids, and statistical plotting parameters. There is no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm use or reconnaissance commands.

low_findings.title: Low Residual Risk: Generated Examples May Touch Local Files
low_findings.description: Some examples show reading a local CSV or saving figures. This is expected for a visualization skill and is user-directed, but users should review paths before running generated code on sensitive datasets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads and writes are explicit plotting examples, not hidden behavior. The risk is limited to user-run code interacting with local data or output files.

content.value_statement: Statistical charts often need careful plot choices, clean styling, and readable comparisons. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce seaborn workflows for exploration, analysis, and publication figures.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use scatter, line, distribution, categorical, regression, matrix, and grid plots.
Provides seaborn examples for pair plots, heatmaps, KDE plots, violin plots, and faceted figures.
Guides use of seaborn.objects for declarative layered visualizations.
Covers themes, contexts, palettes, legends, labels, and publication export settings.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for labels, legends, figure size, palettes, and KDE bandwidth.
Shows how seaborn integrates with pandas data frames and matplotlib axes.
limitations: It does not execute code, load datasets, or verify plots by itself.
Examples assume Python, pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, and sometimes scipy are installed.
It cannot validate private datasets without the user sharing schema or sample data.
It focuses on seaborn and matplotlib, not interactive dashboard libraries.

use_cases.title: Explore a New Dataset
use_cases.description: Create pair plots, distributions, and correlation heatmaps to understand numeric variables and category relationships.

use_cases.title: Build Publication Figures
use_cases.description: Design multi-panel seaborn figures with readable labels, colorblind-safe palettes, confidence intervals, and export settings.

use_cases.title: Generate Plotting Code Faster
use_cases.description: Ask Claude, Codex, or Claude Code for reusable seaborn snippets that match a data frame and analysis goal.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Plot Type
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a pandas data frame with these columns: [columns]. Recommend seaborn plots for exploring relationships, distributions, and outliers.

prompt_templates.title: Create an EDA Figure Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a seaborn exploratory analysis plan for [dataset]. Include pair plots, distribution plots, categorical comparisons, and a correlation heatmap.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing Plot
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this seaborn plotting goal: [goal]. Suggest better encodings, labels, palette choices, legend placement, and export settings.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Publication Figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a multi-panel seaborn figure for [research question]. Include plot types, layout, statistical annotations, accessibility choices, and final export guidance.

output_examples.input: Create a visualization plan for sales by region, product category, and month.
output: Use a line plot for monthly trends by region.
Use a box or violin plot to compare category distributions.
Use a heatmap for region and category totals.
Apply a colorblind-safe palette and clear axis labels.

output_examples.input: Improve a crowded categorical comparison figure.
output: Switch from overlapping points to a box plot with a strip overlay.
Rotate long labels and increase figure width.
Move the legend outside the plotting area.
Use consistent category ordering across panels.

output_examples.input: Prepare a correlation figure for a report.
output: Select numeric columns and compute the correlation matrix.
Mask one triangle to reduce duplicate information.
Use a diverging palette centered at zero.
Export at high resolution with tight bounding boxes.

best_practices: Start with the question, then choose the plot type that exposes the needed comparison.
Use semantic mappings such as hue, style, row, and column only when they add meaning.
Set themes, labels, palettes, and export settings before sharing final figures.
anti_patterns: Do not use pair plots on very wide datasets without selecting relevant variables first.
Do not use decorative palettes that make categories hard to distinguish.
Do not hide raw distributions when summary statistics may obscure important variation.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps create seaborn statistical visualizations, choose plot types, refine styling, and prepare figures for analysis or publication.

faq.question: Does it run Python code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The user or development environment runs any generated code.

faq.question: Can it work with my private data?
faq.answer: Yes, if you describe the columns or share safe sample data. Avoid sharing sensitive records unless necessary.

faq.question: Does it support the seaborn.objects API?
faq.answer: Yes. The reference material explains Plot, marks, stats, moves, facets, scales, labels, themes, and layout.

faq.question: Is this only for notebooks?
faq.answer: No. The examples can guide notebooks, Python scripts, reports, and figure export workflows.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk low?
faq.answer: Static findings were false positives from Markdown and plotting vocabulary. A small residual risk remains for user-run local file examples.
